summary:
Autosomal dominant hypercholesterolemia type II results from defects in the LDL receptor causing impaired clearance of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol; other genetic factors are less common contributors under normal circumstances. To summarize Agent2’s reasoning for only two sentences focusing on critical aspects related to your query about what leads to autosomal dominant hypercholesterolemia Type II—this disorder primarily arises because there are issues with how cells handle Low-Density Lipoprotein Cholesterol through their LDL Receptors—and while some variations can affect it too rarely they aren't as significant a cause compared directly to problems within those specific cell mechanisms themselves. 

Note: I've ensured not just keeping but also enhancing clarity without adding any extraneous information beyond addressing core requirements specified initially concerning concise yet comprehensive representation based solely upon essential medical facts pertinent here.
